title: Commercial Roofing in Marlborough, MA | Commercial Roofers of Worcester

html:
Sizing Roof Budgets for Marlborough's Campus and Distribution Buildings

Marlborough carries some of the largest single-building rooftops in Worcester County. Corporate campuses like the Boston Scientific site and the office-and-distribution cluster along I-495 and Route 20 push individual roof plans into six-figure square footage, and that changes how we scope a job from the first site walk. A leak on a 12,000 square foot retail box gets handled differently than a leak on a 220,000 square foot corporate roof with forty rooftop units and three tenant improvements layered on top of the original membrane.

Rooftop Real Estate Along I-495 and Route 20

The office parks off Route 20 and the interchange at I-495 hold a mix of low-slope built-up roofs from the 1980s and newer TPO installed during the last capital cycle. BJ's Wholesale Club's corporate headquarters and the surrounding business park buildings run large, mechanically fastened membranes with heavy rooftop unit counts, since these are HVAC-intensive office and lab spaces, not simple retail boxes. When we bid a roof in this corridor we walk every unit curb, because a campus building rarely has one roof age or one roof system across its whole footprint. Additions get bolted onto original construction, and the seam between the two is where most of our service calls start.

Distribution and flex-industrial buildings near the Marlborough-Hudson line carry simpler roof geometry but bigger membrane runs. A single TPO field on one of these buildings can exceed 150,000 square feet, which means fastener pattern and seam welding quality control matter more than on a smaller job, since a 2% defect rate on a small roof is a handful of seams and on a roof this size it is hundreds of linear feet.

Snow Load Numbers That Drive the Spec

Worcester County sits in a ground snow load band that Massachusetts building code generally sets between 35 and 50 pounds per square foot depending on elevation and exposure, and Marlborough's rolling terrain means we check the specific parcel rather than assume a flat number. Roof design load runs lower than ground snow load after exposure and thermal factors, but drift loading against parapets and behind rooftop units can spike well above the field load, which is where under-designed older roofs show stress cracking first.

Ice dams on a flat commercial roof work differently than on a pitched residential roof. Heat loss through poorly insulated roof decks melts snow near rooftop units and vent stacks, that water refreezes at the cold perimeter, and ponding builds behind the ice ridge until it finds a seam or a fastener penetration. We see this pattern most on older mechanically fastened EPDM roofs where the insulation has compressed or gotten wet over time.

TPO, EPDM, and the Membrane Decision on a Large Field

For a roof this size, membrane choice is a cost-per-square-foot decision first and a performance decision second, because the difference between systems compounds fast across 100,000-plus square feet. TPO installed runs roughly in the $6 to $9 per square foot range depending on insulation thickness and fastening density, with white membrane giving Marlborough's office tenants a real reduction in rooftop unit run time during summer cooling load. EPDM costs less installed but loses that reflectivity benefit unless you spec white EPDM, which narrows the price gap. On campus buildings with heavy foot traffic for mechanical service, we weight seam durability and walkway pad coverage over sticker price, since a seam failure under a service technician's boot costs more in emergency callouts than the difference in membrane price ever saved.

  • HVAC curb flashing and counterflashing condition at every rooftop unit
  • Walkway pad placement between access hatches and serviced equipment
  • Roof drain and scupper capacity against any tenant improvement additions
  • Perimeter edge metal wind uplift rating for the building's exposure category
  • Structural review before adding solar arrays or additional rooftop mechanical

Recover vs Full Tear-Off on a Corporate Roof

Most of the campus and office-park roofs we evaluate in Marlborough are candidates for recover rather than tear-off, provided the existing insulation is dry and the deck has no structural issues. A recover job over sound EPDM or built-up roofing runs meaningfully cheaper per square foot than tear-off, and over a 20-year term the lifecycle cost often favors recover even with a slightly shorter service life, because you avoid tear-off labor, disposal tonnage fees, and interior protection costs for occupied space below. Where we push back on recover is any roof with wet insulation found during core cuts, since covering wet insulation just buries a bigger repair for later at higher cost.

For distribution buildings that can't have wet insulation sitting under a new membrane without drying, we'll spec mechanically attached tear-off even where the budget number is higher, because the lifecycle math changes once you account for the interior damage risk of trapped moisture under a warehouse roof holding racked inventory.

Common Roofing Questions in Marlborough

How much does a new TPO roof cost per square foot in Marlborough?

Installed pricing typically falls in the $6 to $9 per square foot range for a mechanically fastened system, with insulation thickness, fastening density for wind exposure, and rooftop unit count all moving that number. A firm figure requires a roof plan takeoff, not a phone estimate.

Can a distribution building stay operational during a reroof?

Yes, in most cases. We phase sections and stage material so racked inventory and dock operations continue, and we schedule tear-off sections around weather windows to limit any open-deck exposure time.

What snow load should a Marlborough roof be designed to carry?

Design snow load depends on the parcel's elevation and exposure category under the Massachusetts building code, generally landing in the 35 to 50 pound per square foot ground snow load range for this part of Worcester County before exposure and thermal adjustments are applied.

Do you recover over an existing EPDM roof or does it need to come off?

Recover is possible when core cuts confirm dry insulation and a sound deck. If moisture shows up in the cuts, that section gets torn off and replaced rather than covered.

How often should rooftop mechanical curbs get inspected?

Twice a year at minimum, spring and fall, with an added check after any major storm. Curb flashing failure is the single most common leak source we find on campus buildings with high rooftop unit counts.
